raw_text:
Video
You Need To Quit Weed.

Transcript
Kind: captions Language: en [00:01] Weed is a serious drug that can have  devastating consequences for your life. [00:05] This is an unpopular thing to say but it is true. [00:09] We don’t think it should be illegal  because for most people using weed is [00:12] not problematic or has only  mild negative consequences. [00:16] But about 20% of people who have ever  tried it develop cannabis use disorder, [00:20] and some of them become severely addicted. [00:23] That’s actually a stunning  amount of people and many [00:25] who start using weed don’t  know what they are in for. [00:28] In the US, daily or near daily weed  use has skyrocketed since way before [00:33] legalization and there are now more daily  users of weed than drinkers of alcohol. [00:37] And most daily users consume very high amounts. [00:41] In 2023, 1 in 15 adult Americans were  suffering from some form of cannabis addiction. [00:46] In England, cannabis is by far the  top drug teenagers seek help for. [00:51] Today we’ll ignore medical use,  casual users and people with mild [00:55] problems and instead we focus on the  people who have a serious addiction [00:59] or are in danger of developing one,  even if they are not aware of it. [01:03] Weed is usually discussed in the  context of teenagers because using [01:07] any drug while your brain is melting  and rebuilding itself is a bad idea. [01:12] Instead we’ll have a frank conversation  about how it affects you in your 20s and 30s. [01:17] We will combine research with our  own personal experience of weed [01:20] addiction – unfortunately  we do have plenty of both. [01:24] Please check out our sources but  also know this script is written [01:27] by someone who used weed daily  for 15 years before quitting. [01:32] Ok, so let’s get into it. [01:35] Becoming Addicted By Accident [01:38] Most people slip into  addiction gradually and slowly. [01:41] They start using weed for a variety of reasons, [01:44] to experiment, to cope or unwind from stress,  because it is fun and easily available. [01:49] Weed can make you feel more  alive and things more exciting. [01:52] Music sounds better, movies  are funnier, food is a delight. [01:56] It can be great fun with friends  and a major source of comfort. [01:59] The frequency with which you are consuming  weed is the most obvious thing to look out for. [02:04] If you are using daily or almost daily this  is a clear sign you are developing a problem. [02:09] As a daily user your risk of cannabis use disorder  is up to 30% and certainly a pretty relevant risk, [02:15] since it might take years before  you realize the full weight of it. [02:19] So if you are not careful about it, [02:21] what started out as an occasional  high becomes more and more normalized. [02:26] Bit by bit your weekends revolve around weed  or shift to activities that you can do stoned. [02:31] Often other hobbies or obligations  become less of a priority. [02:35] Then it creeps into most weekdays and finally  it is an established ritual every day. [02:40] Over time, weed’s nature changes. [02:43] The joy enhancing effects dissipate,  while a comfortable numbness takes over. [02:47] Weed can become your primary method  of coping with life but it doesn’t [02:51] make you stronger, better or more resilient. [02:53] Instead it covers up negative emotions by  building a snail shell that you can retreat into. [03:00] Do you have a problem with weed? [03:02] Super easy test: Stop doing it  for four weeks, starting today. [03:06] Not tomorrow, today. [03:08] No matter what you have coming up. [03:09] If that feels challenging or you can’t make  it, this should give you food for thought. [03:14] Once weed has become a daily or near daily habit [03:17] the cumulative damage to  your life can be profound. [03:21] But first, let's talk about another thing that   [03:23] can become harmful without you  noticing it – no data security. [03:28] Today’s sponsor Incogni helps you reduce  your risk of scam, harassment and more. [03:33] Data brokers constantly collect  your valuable personal data,   [03:36] which can quickly fall into the wrong hands. [03:38] The result is lots of spam at best  - or a stolen identity at worst. [03:44] But you often don't even know that your data has  been sold and it’s really hard to get it removed. [03:49] Besides, other brokers will continue  right away so it starts all over again. [03:53] Incogni helps you break the loop. [03:55] Simply give Incogni permission  to work on your behalf, and your [03:58] data will be deleted automatically  and is no longer offered for sale. [04:02] You can also use the custom  removal service to delete [04:05] your data from specific sites that  have exposed it without permission. [04:08] Incogni is the first and only data removal  service that has been verified by Deloitte. [04:13] Use the code KURZGESAGT to protect your  peace of mind and get 60% off annual plans – [04:20] and now back to weed. [04:24] Forever Tomorrow [04:27] For problematic weed users, life can  sometimes progress in slow motion – [04:31] the addiction keeps you where you are, while  your body ages and your friends move forward. [04:36] But youth masks stagnation. [04:39] For years you don’t notice how much  weed might be holding you back, [04:42] because if you are young, life changes by itself – [04:45] the outside world takes on the initiative  and provides a lot of support for you. [04:49] You are surrounded by peers, may still have  childhood friends, progress through school, [04:54] start work or university and  find new places and friendships. [04:58] Moving slowly is kind of ok in  your twenties as life naturally [05:01] throws experiences and challenges at you. [05:04] But it is still not great for you. [05:06] This is the time where your body is  probably at its peak and you can use [05:09] it for things you will not be able to do later. [05:11] From sports to dating and staying out  late, traveling and experiencing the world. [05:16] It is the easiest time to  build a healthy social life, [05:19] the greatest predictor of how happy  you will be over the next few decades. [05:23] You will likely never have fewer responsibilities  again, so you can explore and take risks. [05:29] Spending this time stoned with passive activities [05:31] like scrolling over reposts on reddit  is something you might regret later. [05:36] You’ll never get this period of your life back. [05:39] As you age out of your twenties  things change massively. [05:43] Around this time most people naturally tone  down their weed consumption or quit entirely. [05:47] You are entering one of life's  great inflection points. [05:50] And you risk getting left behind. [05:54] Your Social Life is Different After 30 [05:57] Friendships need care, attention  and active time investment to [06:01] survive and weed addiction works  against you on multiple levels. [06:05] Studies show that for some people weed can worsen [06:07] social anxiety especially if  you start using more and more. [06:11] And once you feel anxious about socializing, [06:14] it becomes pretty tempting to just stay  home and avoid events or new experiences. [06:19] Since weed numbs feelings it’s easy to push  away FOMO or guilt about canceling last minute. [06:25] You’ll go next time!! But will you? [06:28] Speaking from personal experience – you  may turn into a friend that is unreliable, [06:33] flaky or rarely shows up anymore. [06:36] But the more opportunities to hang with friends  you skip, the fewer will be there in the future. [06:41] Especially if you do weed alone it can  become the central focal point in your life. [06:46] Reports show that many people with  addictions gradually reduce the [06:49] activities or hobbies they once  enjoyed in favor of consuming. [06:52] Bit by bit this can make you withdraw  from the people in your life. [06:56] While this can be pretty bad for  your social life in your 20s it   [07:00] may be catastrophic by the time you turn 30. [07:02] Because in their 30s most people are plenty busy. [07:06] Careers, partners, travel, children and  their closest friends are now priorities. [07:11] Free time becomes super valuable and casual hang [07:14] outs turn into two adults  comparing their calendars. [07:17] So people in their 30s are way more selective  about what and who they spend their time with. [07:22] Smoking a joint drops way  down their priority list. [07:26] Even without falling out, you may  simply become incompatible if your [07:30] identity becomes tied up with weed, or when  your friends have a lot going on and you don’t. [07:35] This happens a lot without weed,  but weed can supercharge it. [07:39] So many problematic weed users  focus on a small circle of [07:42] friends and acquaintances who are also using weed. [07:45] This can create an isolation trap: [07:47] If your friends quit or they  withdraw to use it alone, [07:50] you can face sudden isolation and loneliness. [07:53] Or you may feel pressure to keep using it  because you are scared of losing your friends. [07:58] It is harder and takes longer to make  new friends in your 30s than in your 20s, [08:02] so to change your situation you need courage, [08:05] energy and motivation – things weed  addiction makes much harder to muster. [08:10] Worse still, people who feel  lonely tend to use more weed [08:13] to cope – which is linked to  even more social isolation. [08:17] Weed can fill the role of your best  friend. It is there when you are lonely, [08:21] it makes you feel ok in the moment. [08:23] Your love life can also suffer. [08:25] Social anxiety and complacency is a  great motivator to put off dating and [08:29] having sexual experiences in your 20s and it  doesn’t get much easier as time passes. [08:34] In relationships weed addiction  can be extremely disruptive. [08:38] Reports show that partners of  daily users can feel neglected, [08:42] that couple communication can break  down and that trust evaporates. [08:46] The addicted partner usually  doesn’t realize how poorly they [08:49] are handling relationship issues and can  be blindsided when their love leaves them. [08:53] And to be frank, in their 30s people want  a partner who is ready to share the growing [08:57] responsibilities of adulthood and wants to  build a life together – the older you get, [09:02] the more unattractive your  weed addiction makes you. [09:05] Ok but who needs friends or love  if you have a career, right? [09:10] Well… [09:11] The Achievement Delay Machine [09:13] Long term studies show that heavy weed users  are more likely to end up with reduced academic [09:18] performance, a worse education, lower income,  less savings, and less stable employment. [09:24] Even when compared to people from similar  family backgrounds and social class. [09:28] Many people with weed addiction struggle to  show up for work, they’re not productive, [09:32] they procrastinate and they fail  to meet their responsibilities. [09:36] The consequences of missed opportunities  and bad decisions slowly accumulate. [09:40] Maybe you skipped networking events,  had no ambition and didn’t get promoted, [09:44] or hopped between entry-level jobs. [09:47] It is also much harder to build up savings since  the addiction can cost you thousands each year. [09:52] In your 20s you transition from being  useless to being good at something. [09:56] And so in your 30s expectations from  others and yourself change sharply. [10:01] People tend to cut you a lot of slack when you  are young, but this has an expiration date. [10:06] The older you get, the worse it feels to compare [10:08] yourself to peers that are moving on  in life and start achieving things. [10:12] And it is not just career goals, [10:14] if you spend a decade or two using weed  all the time you grow less as a person. [10:18] You probably traveled less,  went out less, met fewer people, [10:22] had fewer interesting experiences and  in a way, lived less than your peers. [10:27] Which also really becomes noticeable in your 30s. [10:30] In some studies, long term chronic weed users  had significantly lower life satisfaction, from [10:35] their motivation and pursuit of personal goals,  social and love life to careers and options. [10:41] Your life may just overall be much less  fun and fulfilling that it could have been. [10:45] On top of all of this your mental health is  the final thing that you may erode over time. [10:51] The Prison of Weed [10:53] What makes weed addiction so devious is how at  first it can seem to improve your mental health. [10:59] It can calm anxiety or depressive feelings,  make you more relaxed and feel less lonely. [11:04] But weed acts on your brain’s reward system  and the effects can flip without you noticing. [11:10] Weed can damage your ability to  regulate your emotions and can [11:13] worsen anxiety or depressive feelings and  can escalate into serious mental disorders. [11:19] Which you might think that you can self  medicate against by doing even more weed. [11:24] This can impair your coping skills,  making you fragile and easily overwhelmed, [11:28] unable to deal with stress, increase  anxiety or depressive symptoms, [11:32] worsen your mood, cause mood  swings and irritability. [11:36] The addiction can hold back a dam of  bottled up negative feelings that is [11:40] ready to crash into you and the  people around you, at any time. [11:43] Until eventually it doesn’t work anymore. [11:46] Especially during a mental health crisis,  weed can make things much worse for you. [11:51] On the flipside there is research  that shows that addicted people who [11:54] give up weed experience a noticeable  improvement in their mental health. [11:58] Here is the thing. [11:59] All the negatives we talked about, missing your  life as it flies by, social isolation and not even [12:05] getting close to living up to your potential are  not happening some time in the future but today. [12:10] No matter your age. [12:11] If you find yourself addicted or in danger  of becoming addicted, there is a way out. [12:16] It is not fun and it will be hard. [12:19] You need to quit. [12:20] And the earlier you do, the  better your life will be.
